problem_addressed: Closing the performance gap between weakly-supervised scribble-based and fully-supervised methods for 3D abdominal organ segmentation in CT images.
core_claim: The proposed TDNet with dual consistency constraints (USPC and MPCC) achieves state-of-the-art performance in scribble-supervised abdominal organ segmentation.
key_contributions: A novel 3D Triple-branch Multi-dilated Network (TDNet) with one encoder and three decoders using dilation rates of 1, 3, and 6 to capture complementary multi-scale features.
Uncertainty-weighted Soft Pseudo label Consistency (USPC) loss to rectify noisy pseudo labels using voxel-wise entropy-based uncertainty weighting.
Multi-view Projection-based Class-similarity Consistency (MPCC) loss to enforce class relationship consistency across axial, sagittal, and coronal views.
novelty: Combines multi-scale feature extraction via TDNet with dual-level consistency constraints (pixel-wise uncertainty weighting and class-wise multi-view projection) to stabilize weakly-supervised learning and improve segmentation accuracy.
method_summary: TDNet uses a shared encoder feeding three decoders with dilation rates 1/3/6. Decoder outputs are averaged into soft pseudo labels, weighted by uncertainty (entropy) via USPC loss, and class affinity matrices are aligned across multi-view projections via MPCC loss, supervised alongside partial cross-entropy on scribbles.
method_components: Triple-branch Multi-dilated Network (TDNet) with dilation rates 1, 3, 6
Uncertainty-weighted Soft Pseudo label Consistency (USPC) loss using entropy-based weights
Multi-view Projection-based Class-similarity Consistency (MPCC) loss using class affinity matrices
Partial Cross-Entropy (pCE) loss for scribble supervision
key_innovation: Voxel-wise uncertainty weighting in USPC loss to suppress unreliable pseudo labels and multi-view projection in MPCC loss to leverage inter-class relationships, jointly stabilizing pseudo-label generation and improving segmentation.
assumptions: Different dilation rates capture complementary multi-scale features
Class affinity matrices computed via multi-view projection encode meaningful inter-class relationships
Entropy-based uncertainty estimation effectively identifies unreliable pseudo labels
theoretical_grounding: None explicitly provided; relies on empirical validation of loss functions and architecture on WORD dataset.
datasets_used: WORD dataset (cervical cancer patients before radiotherapy, 100 training, 20 validation, 30 testing)
hyperparameters: SGD optimizer (momentum 0.9, weight decay 10−4), α=10.0, β=1.0, batch size 1, patch size [80, 96, 96], max iterations 6×104, sliding window inference
headline_result: Average DSC of 85.74±5.22% on WORD testing set
ablations: Triple-branch with different dilation rates improved DSC by 7.70% over baseline
Adding LUSP C improved DSC by 2.24% (82.57% to 84.21%)
Adding LMP CC further improved DSC by 0.54% (84.21% to 84.75%)
Different dilation rates contributed to better performance than same dilation rates
improvement_over_baseline: Average DSC improved by 11.04 percentage points (74.70% to 85.74%), ASD reduced by 23.18mm (25.51mm to 2.33mm), HD95 reduced by 72.14mm (79.98mm to 7.84mm)
stated_limitations: Performance gap between weakly-supervised and fully supervised methods
Limited annotation efficiency compared to fully supervised approaches
Potential challenges with very small or anatomically complex organs
unstated_weaknesses: Limited ablation studies on individual components of the triple-branch network
Potential overfitting to specific dataset characteristics
Lack of comparison with more recent weakly supervised methods
Unclear computational complexity and training requirements
threats_to_validity: Dataset bias - method may not generalize to different CT scanners or protocols
Annotation bias - scribbles may not capture all relevant anatomical variations
Domain shift challenges when applied to different patient populations
open_questions: How does the method perform with different levels of scribble annotation quality?
What is the minimum amount of scribble information needed for acceptable performance?
Can the approach be effectively adapted to other imaging modalities beyond CT?
How does the method handle cases with significant anatomical variations?
future_work: Incorporating uncertainty estimation into the scribble-based framework
Developing more efficient architectures for clinical deployment
Extending the method to other anatomical regions beyond the abdomen
gaps_this_enables: Uncertainty-aware pseudo-label selection for weakly supervised segmentation
Methods to balance pixel- and class-wise consistency in multi-organ segmentation
Approaches to reduce the performance gap between weakly and fully supervised methods
Techniques for handling annotation uncertainty in medical image segmentation
relevance_to_topic: This paper directly relates to uncertainty-aware pseudo-label selection by addressing the challenge of generating reliable segmentation from weakly supervised scribble annotations.
reusable_assets: Pixel- and class-wise consistency loss functions
Multi-organ segmentation pipeline for abdominal CT
best_result: Average DSC of 85.74±5.22% on WORD testing set
limitations_stated: Performance gap between weakly-supervised and fully supervised methods
Limited annotation efficiency compared to fully supervised approaches
Potential challenges with very small or anatomically complex organs
title: Scribble-based 3D Multiple Abdominal Organ Segmentation via Triple-branch Multi-dilated Network with Pixel- and Class-wise Consistency
